title: Git & GitHub Cheat Sheet
text:
Command
What it does




git config --global user.name "Name"
Set your name for commits


git config --global user.email "email"
Set your email for commits


git init
Create a new repository in the current folder


git clone <url>
Download a remote repository to your computer

title: Git & GitHub Cheat Sheet
text:
Command
What it does




git config --global user.name "Name"
Set your name for commits


git config --global user.email "email"
Set your email for commits


git init
Create a new repository in the current folder


git clone <url>
Download a remote repository to your computer

title: Git & GitHub Cheat Sheet
text:
Daily Workflow







Command
What it does




git status
Show which files are modified, staged, or untracked


git add <file>
Stage a file for the next commit


git add .
Stage all changed files


git commit -m "message"
Save a snapshot with a descriptive message


git diff
Show unstaged changes


git diff --staged
Show staged changes (what will be committed)


git log --oneline
View commit history (compact)


git log
View commit history (detailed)

title: Git & GitHub Cheat Sheet
text:
Undoing Things







Command
What it does




git restore --staged <file>
Unstage a file (keep edits)


git restore <file>
Discard edits, revert to last commit (destructive)


git reset --soft HEAD~1
Undo last commit (keep files staged)

title: Git & GitHub Cheat Sheet
text:
Remotes (GitHub)



Command
What it does




git remote add origin <url>
Link local repo to GitHub


git push -u origin main
Push commits to GitHub (first time)


git push
Push commits (after -u is set)


git pull origin main
Download new commits from GitHub

title: Git & GitHub Cheat Sheet
Git & GitHub Cheat Sheet
text:
Tags (Reproducibility)



Command
What it does




git tag -a v1.0 -m "msg"
Create an annotated tag


git push --tags
Push all tags to GitHub


git checkout v1.0
Check out a specific tagged version

title: Git & GitHub Cheat Sheet
text:
SSH Setup (One-Time)
# Generate key
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "you@email.com"

# Start agent and add key
eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Copy public key (paste into GitHub Settings > SSH Keys)
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub

# Test connection
ssh -T git@github.com

title: Git & GitHub Cheat Sheet
text:
.gitignore Template for R Projects
# Data files
*.csv
*.dta
*.rds
*.xlsx
data/

# R artifacts
.Rhistory
.RData
.Rproj.user/

# Output (can be regenerated)
*.pdf
*.html
output/

# System files
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db

title: Version Control with Git & GitHub
text:
This two-session workshop introduces version control using Git and GitHub. The target audience is PhD students in applied economics with no prior experience with the command line or Git.
By the end of these sessions you will be able to:

Track changes to your R scripts and research code using Git
Undo mistakes and compare versions of your work
Back up your code on GitHub
Collaborate with co-authors using branches and pull requests
Tag specific versions of your code for reproducibility

title: Version Control with Git & GitHub
text:
This two-session workshop introduces version control using Git and GitHub. The target audience is PhD students in applied economics with no prior experience with the command line or Git.
By the end of these sessions you will be able to:

Track changes to your R scripts and research code using Git
Undo mistakes and compare versions of your work
Back up your code on GitHub
Collaborate with co-authors using branches and pull requests
Tag specific versions of your code for reproducibility

title: Version Control with Git & GitHub
text:
Sessions
Session 1: Git Fundamentals. Learn the core Git workflow on your own computer: init, add, commit, log, diff, .gitignore, and undoing mistakes.
Session 2: GitHub & Collaboration. Connect to GitHub, push and pull code, create branches, open pull requests, and tag releases for reproducibility.

title: Version Control with Git & GitHub
text:
Before You Begin
Please complete the following before the first session:

Install Git on your laptop

Mac: brew install git (if you have Homebrew) or download from git-scm.com
Windows: download the installer from git-scm.com

Open a terminal

Mac: open the Terminal app (search for “Terminal” in Spotlight)
Windows: open Git Bash (installed with Git)

Verify the installation by running:

git --version
You should see something like git version 2.39.0.
Before Session 2, also create a free GitHub account at github.com.

title: Version Control with Git & GitHub
text:
Companion Materials

Session 1 slides (PowerPoint)
Session 2 slides (PowerPoint)
Exercise R scripts

title: Session 2: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
In Session 1 we learned the core local Git workflow:

git init: create a repository
git add / git commit: stage and save snapshots
git log / git diff: view history and changes
.gitignore: exclude files from tracking
git restore / git reset: undo mistakes

Today we take those skills online.

title: Session 2: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
In Session 1 we learned the core local Git workflow:

git init: create a repository
git add / git commit: stage and save snapshots
git log / git diff: view history and changes
.gitignore: exclude files from tracking
git restore / git reset: undo mistakes

Today we take those skills online.

title: Session 2: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
What Is GitHub?
GitHub is a cloud hosting service for Git repositories. It provides:

Backup: your code lives in the cloud, not just on your laptop
Collaboration: co-authors can contribute to the same project
Sharing: publish replication packages and make research transparent







Note



GitHub is free for public and private repositories. You can also apply for GitHub Education benefits as a student or faculty member.

title: Session 2: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Connect to GitHub: SSH Keys
SSH keys let your computer authenticate with GitHub without typing a password every time.

1. Generate a key pair
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "you@email.com"
Press Enter to accept the default file location. You can set a passphrase or leave it empty.


2. Start the SSH agent and add your key
eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519


3. Copy the public key
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
Copy the entire output.


4. Add the key to GitHub

Go to github.com → click your profile picture → Settings
In the left sidebar, click SSH and GPG keys
Click New SSH key
Paste your key and give it a title (e.g., “My Laptop”)
Click Add SSH key



5. Test the connection
ssh -T git@github.com
You should see: Hi username! You've successfully authenticated...






Tip



Windows users: run these commands in Git Bash, not in PowerShell or Command Prompt.

title: Session 2: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Remotes: Push and Pull
A remote is a copy of your repository stored on a server (like GitHub). The standard remote name is origin.

The relationship
Your Computer  ←— git pull —→  GitHub (origin)
               —— git push ——→

git push uploads your commits to GitHub
git pull downloads new commits from GitHub



Link your local repo to GitHub
First, create a new repository on GitHub:

Go to github.com/new
Name it (e.g., wage-analysis)
Do NOT initialize with a README (your local repo already has commits)
Click Create repository

GitHub will show you setup commands. Use the SSH option:
git remote add origin git@github.com:yourusername/wage-analysis.git


Push your commits
git push -u origin main
The -u flag sets origin main as the default upstream, so future pushes only need git push.


Pull changes
If someone else pushed commits (or you edited a file on GitHub), download them:
git pull origin main

title: Session 2: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Cloning a Repository
git clone downloads an entire repository (with full history) from GitHub to your computer:
git clone git@github.com:username/wage-analysis.git
cd wage-analysis
After cloning, the remote origin is already configured. You can immediately push and pull.






Tip



Cloning is how you start working on a collaborator’s project. You get the full history, all branches, and a ready-to-go remote connection.

title: Session 2: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Exercise 3: Push to GitHub
Time: ~15 minutes

Create a repository on GitHub. Go to github.com/new, name it wage-analysis, and click Create (do NOT initialize with README).
Add the remote to your local wage-analysis project:

cd wage-analysis
git remote add origin git@github.com:YOURUSERNAME/wage-analysis.git
Replace YOURUSERNAME with your GitHub username.

Push your commits:

git push -u origin main

Verify. Go to your repository page on GitHub and refresh. You should see your files and commit history.
Test pulling. On GitHub, click on clean_data.R → pencil icon → add a comment line at the top → Commit. Then pull locally:

git pull origin main
Open the file to confirm the edit is there.

title: Session 2: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Branches
A branch is a parallel version of your code. You can experiment on a branch without affecting the main version. When the work is ready, you merge it back.

Why branches?

Try a new specification without breaking working code
Each co-author works on their own branch
Keep main clean and functional at all times



Create and switch to a branch
git branch add-iv-analysis
git checkout add-iv-analysis
Or, as a shortcut:
git checkout -b add-iv-analysis


Work on the branch
Make your edits, stage, and commit as usual. These commits only exist on the branch.
# Create a new file
echo '# IV regression using 2SLS' > iv_analysis.R
git add iv_analysis.R
git commit -m "Add IV analysis script"


Switch back to main
git checkout main
Notice that iv_analysis.R disappears. It only exists on the other branch. Your working directory reflects whichever branch you are on.


Merge the branch
When you are satisfied with the work on your branch:
git checkout main
git merge add-iv-analysis
Now iv_analysis.R appears on main and all the branch’s commits are part of main’s history.


Delete the branch (optional)
After merging, you can clean up:
git branch -d add-iv-analysis

title: Session 2: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Pull Requests
A pull request (PR) is GitHub’s way of proposing changes. Instead of merging locally, you push a branch to GitHub and ask for it to be reviewed before merging.

The pull request workflow

Create a branch and make your commits
Push the branch to GitHub:

git push -u origin add-iv-analysis

Open a PR on GitHub. Go to your repository → you will see a banner saying “add-iv-analysis had recent pushes” → click Compare & pull request
Write a description explaining what you changed and why
Review. Your co-author reads the changes, leaves comments, and approves
Merge. Click Merge pull request on GitHub







Note



Pull requests are the standard collaboration workflow in both industry and academic research. They create a written record of what changed and why, which is valuable for reproducibility.

title: Session 2: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Handling Merge Conflicts
A merge conflict happens when two people edit the same line in the same file. Git marks the conflict and asks you to choose.

What a conflict looks like
<<<<<<< HEAD
lm(wage ~ educ + exper, data = df)
=======
lm(log(wage) ~ educ + exper + tenure, data = df)
>>>>>>> add-iv-analysis

The top section (HEAD) is your current branch’s version
The bottom section is the incoming branch’s version



How to resolve

Open the file in your editor
Decide which version to keep (or combine them into something new)
Delete the <<<<<<<, =======, and >>>>>>> markers
Stage and commit the resolved file:

git add clean_data.R
git commit -m "Resolve merge conflict in regression specification"






Tip



Merge conflicts are normal, not dangerous. They happen whenever two people edit the same line. The fix is always: open the file, choose the right version, remove the markers, commit.

title: Session 2: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Exercise 4: Branches and Pull Requests
Time: ~20 minutes. Work with a partner.

Setup

Person A: go to your wage-analysis repo on GitHub → Settings → Collaborators → Add your partner’s GitHub username.
Person B: accept the invitation (check your email or GitHub notifications), then clone Person A’s repo:

git clone git@github.com:PERSONA_USERNAME/wage-analysis.git
cd wage-analysis


Create branches and PRs

Both partners: create a branch with your name, add a new .R file, commit, and push:

git checkout -b yourname-feature
Create a file (e.g., descriptive_stats.R or robustness_check.R) with a few lines of R code.
git add your_file.R
git commit -m "Add descriptive statistics"
git push -u origin yourname-feature

Both partners: go to GitHub and open a pull request from your branch into main.
Review each other’s PR. Click on “Files changed” to see the diff. Leave a comment (e.g., “Looks good!” or “Maybe add a header comment?”). Then click Merge pull request.
Pull the merged changes locally:

git checkout main
git pull origin main
Both files should now be on main.

title: Session 2: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Tags for Reproducibility
Tags mark a specific point in history. Use them to record the exact code that produced a paper’s results.

Create a tag
git tag -a v1.0 -m "Code for AER submission"


Push tags to GitHub
git push --tags


Check out a tag later
git checkout v1.0
This puts you in “detached HEAD” state. You can look around and run code, but any new commits won’t belong to a branch. To go back:
git checkout main


GitHub Releases
On GitHub, tags appear under the Releases tab. You can attach files (data documentation, README) to a release, making it a self-contained replication package.






Important



Many journals now require replication packages. A tagged GitHub release with a README is a clean, standard way to comply.

title: Session 2: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Summary
You now have the tools to:



Task
Commands




Track changes locally
git init, add, commit


View history
git log, diff


Back up to GitHub
git remote add, push, pull


Collaborate
git branch, checkout, merge


Review changes
Pull requests on GitHub


Mark reproducible versions
git tag




Next steps

Put your current research project on GitHub today
Use branches for every new analysis or robustness check
Write meaningful commit messages
Bookmark this companion website for reference



Resources

Pro Git book (free online)
GitHub Docs
Happy Git with R by Jenny Bryan: an excellent guide for R users

title: Session 1: Git Fundamentals
text:
Version control is a systematic record of how your code changes. It matters in three situations every applied economist now faces.


If your project folder has ever looked like this:
analysis_v1.R
analysis_v2.R
analysis_FINAL.R
analysis_FINAL_v2.R
analysis_REALLY_FINAL.R
analysis_FINAL_USE_THIS.R
…then you already know the cost. You made choices you cannot reconstruct. You changed something that worked and cannot remember what.
Git replaces this chaos with a clean history of snapshots. Each snapshot (called a commit) records exactly what changed, when, and why. You can return to any previous version at any time.



Increasingly you will write research code alongside AI coding assistants: Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Codex, and similar tools. These systems produce dozens of lines in seconds. Most of it is useful; some of it is subtly wrong in ways you will not catch on first read.
Version control is what makes this workflow safe. Every AI-assisted change becomes a commit you can review before accepting, revert cleanly when a test breaks three hours later, or roll back selectively while keeping the parts that worked. It also produces a written record of what came from a model and what you wrote yourself. That record is increasingly expected by journal disclosure policies and by AEA replication review.



Applied economics papers increasingly have two, three, or four co-authors writing code together. Someone edits the cleaning script on Monday; someone else reworks the regression specification on Tuesday. Without version control, the only way to combine those edits is to email files back and forth. That process silently destroys work.
Git (and GitHub, which we cover next session) makes collaboration explicit. Every change is attributed to a specific author, every conflict between edits is surfaced rather than overwritten, and every co-author sees the same history.






NoteThese three motivations connect the whole course



Yourself over time is the reproducibility theme (Sessions 2–3 with Lars). AI tools are Sessions 6–7. Co-authors are Session 2 (GitHub). Version control is the connective infrastructure that makes all three workable.

title: Session 1: Git Fundamentals
text:
What Is Git?
Git is a distributed version control system. It takes a snapshot of your entire project every time you commit. Think of it as a timeline:
v1: Load data  →  v2: Clean variables  →  v3: Run regression
Each point on the timeline is a commit. Git stores the full history locally on your computer.






Important



Git ≠ GitHub. Git is the tool that runs on your computer. GitHub is a cloud hosting service where you can store and share your Git repositories online. We cover GitHub in Session 2.

title: Session 1: Git Fundamentals
text:
Setup: Install and Configure

Install Git
Mac (via Homebrew):
brew install git
Windows: download from git-scm.com and run the installer. Use the default options. This also installs Git Bash, a terminal you will use for all commands.


Verify
git --version


Configure your identity
Git attaches your name and email to every commit. Set these once:
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@email.com"
Replace "Your Name" and "you@email.com" with your actual name and email.

title: Session 1: Git Fundamentals
text:
The Three Areas of Git
Every Git project has three areas. Understanding them is the key mental model:








Area
What it is
How you interact




Working Directory
The files you see and edit
You edit files normally


Staging Area
A holding zone for the next commit
git add moves files here


Repository
The permanent history of commits
git commit saves a snapshot



The workflow is: edit → stage → commit.

title: Session 1: Git Fundamentals
text:
Your First Repository

1. Create a project folder
mkdir my-research
cd my-research


2. Initialize Git
git init
This creates a hidden .git/ folder inside your project. That folder stores all version history. You never need to touch it directly.


3. Check the status
git status
Git will tell you that you are on branch main and there are no commits yet. This is your starting point.

title: Session 1: Git Fundamentals
text:
Staging and Committing

Create a file
Open your text editor and create a file called clean_data.R with the following content:
# clean_data.R
# Load and clean the wage dataset

library(readr)

wages <- read_csv("data/wages.csv")

# Remove observations with missing wages
wages <- wages[!is.na(wages$wage), ]

# Log transformation
wages$log_wage <- log(wages$wage)
Save the file in your my-research folder.


Stage the file






NoteThree interfaces, same Git





From here on, command blocks appear in tabs: Terminal, RStudio, and VS Code. All three do exactly the same thing under the hood. Pick whichever you prefer day-to-day. In class we walk through the Terminal tab on the projector.




TerminalRStudioVS Code


git add clean_data.R


In the Git pane (top-right of the IDE by default) → tick the checkbox next to clean_data.R under the Staged column.
If you do not see a Git pane: the project must be an RStudio Project (.Rproj) inside a Git repository. Tools → Project Options → Git/SVN → Version control system: Git, then restart RStudio.


Open the Source Control panel (branch icon in the left activity bar, or ⌃⇧G / Ctrl+Shift+G). Under Changes, hover over clean_data.R and click the + icon. The file moves to Staged Changes.



All three do the same thing: they tell Git “include this file in the next commit.”


Check the status again
git status
You should see clean_data.R listed under “Changes to be committed.”


Commit
git commit -m "Add data cleaning script"
The -m flag lets you write the commit message inline. Every commit needs a message that explains why you made the change.


Make another change and commit
Edit clean_data.R. For example, add a new line:
# Keep only full-time workers
wages <- wages[wages$hours >= 35, ]
Then stage and commit:
git add clean_data.R
git commit -m "Filter to full-time workers only"






TipWriting good commit messages



Be specific. “Fix bug” is unhelpful. “Fix off-by-one error in sample selection” is useful. Your future self (and your co-authors) will thank you.

title: Session 1: Git Fundamentals
section: Exercise 1: Create a Project and Make Commits
text:
Exercise 1: Create a Project and Make Commits
Time: ~15 minutes

Create a folder called wage-analysis and initialize Git:

mkdir wage-analysis
cd wage-analysis
git init

Create a file called clean_data.R. Write a few lines of R code (anything is fine: loading a library, creating a variable, etc.).
Stage and commit:

git add clean_data.R
git commit -m "Add data cleaning script"

Edit the file (add a comment or a new line of code), then stage and commit again:

git add clean_data.R
git commit -m "Add full-time worker filter"

View your commits:

git log
You should see two commits with your messages, timestamps, and author info.

title: Session 1: Git Fundamentals
text:
Viewing History

git log
git log shows the full history of commits, most recent first. Each entry includes the commit hash (a unique ID), author, date, and message.
For a compact view:
git log --oneline
Output looks like:
a1b2c3d  Add regression output table
e4f5g6h  Clean variable names
i7j8k9l  Add data cleaning script


git diff
git diff shows what changed since the last commit (for unstaged files):
git diff
Example output:
- lm(wage ~ educ, data = df)
+ lm(wage ~ educ + exper, data = df)
Lines starting with - were removed. Lines starting with + were added.
To see changes that are already staged:
git diff --staged

title: Session 1: Git Fundamentals
text:
.gitignore
Not every file belongs in version control. Large data files, sensitive credentials, and system files should be excluded.
Create a file called .gitignore in your project root:
# Data files (too large or sensitive for Git)
*.csv
*.dta
data/

# R artifacts
.Rhistory
.RData
.Rproj.user/

# System files
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db
Then stage and commit it:
git add .gitignore
git commit -m "Add .gitignore for data and R artifacts"






Warning



Data files do not belong in Git. Git is designed for code (small text files), not for large datasets. If you commit a 500 MB CSV, every collaborator will have to download it with the full repository. Use a shared drive, Dropbox, or a data repository for large files.

title: Session 1: Git Fundamentals
text:
Undoing Mistakes
You will make mistakes. Git has tools to recover from each of them.

Unstage a file
You staged a file by accident and want to remove it from the staging area (without deleting the file):
git restore --staged clean_data.R


Discard local changes
You edited a file and want to go back to the last committed version:
git restore clean_data.R






Caution



This is destructive. Your edits are gone. Git cannot recover them because they were never committed.




Undo the last commit (keep changes)
You committed too early and want to undo the commit but keep the files staged:
git reset --soft HEAD~1
HEAD~1 means “one commit before the current one.”

title: Session 1: Git Fundamentals
text:
Exercise 2: Full Workflow Practice
Time: ~15 minutes

In your wage-analysis project, create a .gitignore file with the contents shown above. Stage and commit it.
Create a new file called run_regression.R:

# run_regression.R
# Estimate wage equation

library(readr)

wages <- read_csv("data/wages.csv")

# Basic OLS
model1 <- lm(log(wage) ~ educ + exper + tenure, data = wages)
summary(model1)
Stage and commit:
git add run_regression.R
git commit -m "Add basic wage regression"

Edit run_regression.R and add a second model:

# Add experience squared
model2 <- lm(log(wage) ~ educ + exper + I(exper^2) + tenure, data = wages)
summary(model2)
View the diff before committing:
git diff
Then stage and commit:
git add run_regression.R
git commit -m "Add quadratic experience specification"

Practice unstaging. Create a junk file, stage it, then unstage:

echo "test" > junk.R
git add junk.R
git status
git restore --staged junk.R
git status

View your full history:

git log --oneline

title: Session 1: Git Fundamentals
text:
What’s Next
In Session 2 we will connect your local repository to GitHub, learn to push and pull, create branches, and collaborate with pull requests.
Before next session: create a free account at github.com.
